title:
DRFU: Federated Unlearning with Reverse
KL-Divergence-based Knowledge Distillation
abstract:
The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs)
into Federated Learning (FL) has presented an opportunity to
access and leverage private datasets while still upholding data
confidentiality. However, strict adherence to evolving privacy
legislation, most notably the ”Right to be Forgotten” (RTBF),
which mandates the permanent removal of specific user data
upon request, poses a significant challenge. This creates a
dichotomy where exact retraining is computationally prohibitive
for high-dimensional models such as LLMs, while conventional
methods usually cause catastrophic forgetting problems, thereby
eroding the global model’s generalization capabilities. Existing
federated unlearning approaches often struggle to address post-
training, sample-level unlearning requests where only a specific
fraction of a client’s data must be erased after the global model
has converged, failing to balance the effective elimination of
targeted knowledge with the preservation of general utility, all
while incurring prohibitive computational and communication
overheads. To bridge this gap, this paper introduces a novel
Federated Unlearning framework specifically designed for the
unique constraints of LLMs, called Delta Regularized Federated
Unlearning (DRFU), that integrates an adapted Reverse KL-
Divergence-based Knowledge Distillation (RKLD) mechanism
into the FL system. Our method operates in two phases: local
unlearning on the client side to suppress targeted data influence,
followed by a targeted global update using task vector arithmetic.
This design not only enables precise data erasure and high utility
retention but also significantly mitigates communication over-
head and server-side memory bottlenecks. Extensive empirical
evaluations demonstrate that DRFU outperforms state-of-the-
art baselines, achieving a 15.4% to 32.5% relative reduction in
forgetting probability while simultaneously enhancing the global
model’s general utility by up to 52.0%.

title:
FedPPAN: Federated Learning with Privacy
Preserving Adversarial Network
venue: IEEE/ACM International Symposium on Quality of Service (IWQoS) 2026
abstract:
Federated Learning (FL) is increasingly used for
distributed data analytics and on-device intelligence, but its
quality of service (QoS) is often undermined by privacy leakage:
model updates exchanged during training can be exploited by
inference attacks to reveal sensitive attributes and, in some cases,
reconstruct training samples. This paper targets privacy robust-
ness as a first-class QoS dimension for federated training and pro-
poses FedPPAN, a client-side privacy-preserving framework that
integrates a lightweight Privacy-Preserving Adversarial Network
(PPAN) to transform local updates before communication. PPAN
is trained adversarially to minimize information leakage while
preserving convergence and predictive performance, enabling a
controllable privacy–utility operating point without modifying
the global aggregation rule. We evaluate FedPPAN on benchmark
vision datasets under non-IID client distributions and quantify
QoS using (i) model accuracy and convergence behavior, and
(ii) privacy exposure measured via mutual-information-based
leakage metrics under inference attacks. Results show that
FedPPAN achieves a consistently better privacy–utility trade-off
than DP-SGD, reducing leakage substantially while maintaining
comparable accuracy; in our experiments, FedPPAN improves
performance by up to 6% over high-security DP and up to 48%
over low-security DP at similar accuracy levels. These findings
indicate that adversarially protected updates can enhance the
dependability and robustness of FL-based analytics in edge and
distributed systems.

title:
co-GRFuzz: A Lightweight Reinforcement Learning
Framework for Adaptive and Scalable Fuzzing in
Python Libraries
authors: Truong X. Tran, Minh-Viet-Anh Le, Huy Hieu Nguyen, Hai Anh Tran
abstract:
Context: Fuzzing is a widely used automated technique for detecting software
vulnerabilities. However, most coverage-guided fuzzers are optimized for
compiled languages and rely on static instrumentation or replay buffers that
do not suit Python’s dynamic execution model. These limitations hinder
scalability and reduce the effectiveness of fuzzing for modern Python libraries.
Objective: This work aims to improve Python fuzzing by integrating lightweight
reinforcement learning into the mutation process while keeping memory us-
age low. We introduce co-GRFuzz, an enhanced and parallel extension of
our previous framework GRFuzz, designed to support scalable multi-parallel-
process fuzzing for a single target program.
Method: co-GRFuzz employs the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)
algorithm, removing the need for a critic network or replay buffer. A neu-
ral policy estimates mutation heatmaps for input seeds, and two synchro-
nized fuzzing processes share a common Corpus through an Observer com-
ponent. The framework is implemented entirely in Python and evaluated on
five widely used Python libraries under unified experimental conditions.
Results: co-GRFuzz achieves up to 14.8% higher coverage and approximately
32% lower memory consumption compared to RL-based baselines such as PyRainFuzz and PyDQNfuzz. Corpus synchronization across processes en-
hances input diversity and allows broader exploration of execution paths.
The system also uncovered two previously unknown bugs, demonstrating its
practical vulnerability-finding capability.
Conclusion: By combining GRPO-guided mutation with cooperative parallel
fuzzing, co-GRFuzz provides an efficient, scalable, and resource-conscious so-
lution for fuzzing Python libraries. The framework establishes a solid founda-
tion for advancing intelligent, adaptive, and multi-process fuzzing techniques
in software testing.

title: Applying Federated Learning For Encrypted Traffic Classification In Multi-domain Software Defined Network
venue: THE 18th NATIONAL CONFERENCE ON FUNDAMENTAL AND APPLIED INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY RESEARCH
authors: Huy-Hieu Nguyen, Quang-Duy Hoang, Hoang-Hai Dang, Hoang-Viet Tran, Nam-Thang Hoang, Hai-Anh Tran
abstract: Applying Federated Learning For Encrypted Traffic Classification In Multi-domain Software Defined Network

title:
FedEABoost: A Client Entropy Adaptive
Boosting Framework for Federated Learning
venue: Proceedings of the 14th International Symposium on Information and Communication Technology
authors:
Hai Anh Tran, Huy-Hieu Nguyen, Quoc-Trung Le, Abdelhamid
Mellouk, and Truong X. Tran
abstract:
Federated Learning (FL) enables distributed training of ma-
chine learning models across decentralized devices without sharing raw
data, thus providing privacy-preserving solutions suitable for various ap-
plications. However, FL is significantly hindered by data heterogeneity
across clients, known as the non-IID problem. This leading to degraded
model performance, slower convergence, and challenges in achieving fair-
ness across client models. Existing methods typically struggle to ade-
quately handle class imbalance, particularly when certain classes are un-
derrepresented locally. In this paper, we propose FedEABoost, a novel
FL framework integrating AdaBoost training principles to address the
non-IID issue effectively. FedEABoost generates multiple local models in
each client by iteratively emphasizing harder to classify samples, often
from minority classes and then employs an entropy-based selection mech-
anism to choose the most suitable local models for aggregation. Exten-
sive evaluations conducted on Fashion-MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets
demonstrate that FedEABoost outperforms conventional FL baselines,
achieving up to 25% improvement in global model accuracy across var-
ious non-IID scenarios. Our results highlight the robustness and effec-
tiveness of FedEABoost in mitigating the impact of data heterogeneity,
promoting stable convergence, and enhancing generalization.

title: MTMLD-AWSR: A Novel Multi-Teacher Multi-Level Distillation Approach for Class Incremental Learning in Edge-Cloud Systems
authors: Phuong-Thao Nguyen, Hai-Anh Tran, Huy-Hieu Nguyen, Nam-Thang Hoang, Tulika Mandal, Ruthvik Annareddy, Prithvi Choudhary, Truong X Tran
abstract: In practical Edge-Cloud applications, deep learning models often face challenges when learning from continuously generated new data and labels from IoT devices. As a result, the model tends to struggle to retain previous knowledge while absorbing new knowledge, especially information from a new label in a classification task. This problem is also known as Class Incremental Learning (CIL). Among the approaches for CIL, Knowledge Distillation (KD) has emerged as a promising method and has achieved notable success. However, this approach may not effectively capture long-term dependencies and diverse feature representations, leading to suboptimal performance. This paper introduces the Multi-Teacher Multi-level Distillation (MTMLD) into CIL, utilizing multiple teachers to improve knowledge transfer at different levels, including feature, logit, and attention. Additionally, to ensure efficiency and scalability, we propose two novel modules: (1) a Student Reusability Module, which allows the student model to serve as a teacher in subsequent tasks, reducing redundancy, and (2) a Teacher Management Module, which regulates the number of teachers to prevent unnecessary computational overhead. Extensive experiments comparing our method to other algorithms on multiple datasets demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of accuracy and reduction of forgetting, achieving improvements of up to 2.15 % compared to state-of-the-art algorithms on the CIFAR-100 dataset.

title: Efficient Federated Learning Convergence with Epoch Adaptation
venue: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Information Reuse and Integration and Data Science (IRI)
authors: Huy-Hieu Nguyen, Nam-Thang Hoang, Hai-Anh Tran, Tulika Mandal, Ruthvik Annareddy, Prithvi Choudhary, Truong X Tran
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) is well-suited for the Internet of Things and Cloud Computing due to its ability to preserve data privacy, handle large-scale deployments, work with resource-constrained devices, and integrate with edge computing architectures. However, practical FL often encounters heterogeneity problems that stem from the different nature of device resources and user usage patterns. These issues can slow down model convergence, requiring the model to undergo more communication rounds to reach final convergence and causing the training time of a communication round to be prolonged. In this paper, we propose a new Epoch Adaptation Mechanism for Efficient Convergence framework for the FL mechanism to address these heterogeneities. By calculating a suitable number of local epochs for each client based on its computation and communication time in a training round, our method can mitigate the waiting time caused by system heterogeneity by increasing the number of epochs in faster clients. This strategy helps accelerate model convergence without extending the training time in each round. In addition, a double-sided mechanism is applied to our framework to prevent the possibility of overfitting during the training stage. Experimental results show that our framework can boost the convergence of the global model in statistical heterogeneity by up to 80 % in EMNIST dataset and 35 % in CIFAR-10 dataset.

title: GRFuzz: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach to Python Library Fuzzing with GRPO
venue: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Information Reuse and Integration and Data Science (IRI)
authors: Viet-Anh Le-Minh, Hai-Anh Tran, Huy-Hieu Nguyen, Nam-Thang Hoang, Truong X Tran
abstract: In the digital realm, ensuring the security and reliability of systems and software is of paramount importance. Fuzzing has emerged as one of the most effective testing techniques for uncovering vulnerabilities by systematically generating and executing test cases to explore unexpected software behaviors. While numerous studies have explored machine learningenhanced fuzzing for C libraries, research on applying machine learning techniques to fuzzing Python libraries remains limited. Given Python's widespread adoption in critical applications such as web development, data science, and cybersecurity, improving fuzzing efficiency for Python libraries is crucial for strengthening software security. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to fuzzing Python libraries using deep reinforcement learning (DRL), specifically leveraging the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm. Unlike traditional fuzzing methods that rely on random or heuristic-based input generation, our method dynamically learns and prioritizes test cases that maximize code coverage. To further enhance fuzzing effectiveness, we employ a greedy optimization strategy to fine-tune key hyperparameters, ensuring optimal performance. Our results demonstrate a 2.27% - 10.24% improvement in code coverage compared to both PPO-based fuzzing and the traditional Pythonfuzz, and a 28.50% - 33.25% reduction in memory consumption compared to PPO-based fuzzing. These results show that GRPO-based fuzzing can improve vulnerability detection while maintaining lower resource consumption.

title: A new transfer learning-based traffic classification algorithm for a multi-domain sdn network
venue: Proceedings of the 12th International Symposium on Information and Communication Technology
authors: Nam-Thang Hoang, Cong-Son Duong, Minh-Ngoc Vu, Huy-Hieu Nguyen, Xuan-Truong Tran, Van Tong, Hai Anh Tran
abstract: To enhance the efficiency and resource utilization of a computer network, it is imperative to classify network traffic and implement distinct priority policies. Network traffic classification plays a pivotal role across various domains, including network administration, cybersecurity, and network resource optimization. As encrypted network data undergoes diverse evolution, as evident in datasets from tech giants like Google, Facebook, and YouTube, traditional traffic classification methods have given way to machine learning-based approaches. Given that computer networks are primarily deployed as distributed multi-domain systems, employing machine learning for traffic classification becomes challenging when a new network domain appears with a limited dataset. One potential remedy is to employ transfer learning, allowing knowledge transfer from a pre-trained model in an established domain to a new one. In this paper, we present two contributions. First, a novel algorithm called Multi-class TrAdaBoost-CNN is introduced to tackle the challenge of cross-domain classification in encrypted network services. This algorithm extends the Multi-class TrAdaBoost approach by incorporating a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) as a weak learner. Secondly, extensive experiments are conducted on two distinct domains characterized by imbalanced data distributions to assess the efficacy of our proposed method. The experimental results clearly demonstrate that our algorithm outperforms the traditional CNN model, achieving remarkable accuracy improvements of up to 16%, even when dealing with extremely limited data.
